segments.caption: The video opens with a time-lapse shot of the bustling Mall of America, showcasing crowds moving across multiple levels, with escalators and storefronts visible. The scene transitions to a close-up of a person in a yellow shirt inserting a credit card into a payment terminal, followed by a man in a gray suit speaking directly to the camera against a plain background. The narrative continues with a man in a black suit walking through a crowded city street, then cuts to a close-up of a hand swiping a credit card at a point-of-sale terminal. Next, a person holding a child inserts a card at a grocery store checkout, with fruit visible in the background. The video then shows a man in a dark suit and glasses speaking to the camera in a well-lit room. An aerial view of a city neighborhood appears, overlaid with the CNBC logo and the title 'How credit cards work in the U.S.' The segment concludes with a money-counting machine processing cash, displaying '$6.7 trillion' on screen, followed by a busy shopping street and a graphic stating 'More than 60% of those purchases were made using Visa,' with a close-up of a Chase Visa card and a montage of various Visa cards over a dollar bill.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Visa card being tapped on a payment terminal, highlighting the brand's presence in everyday transactions. The scene transitions to Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting, wearing a black suit with a red patterned tie. The narrative then shifts to an exterior shot of the Department of Justice building, followed by a close-up of the FBI seal, establishing a regulatory context. A payment terminal is shown with a hand inserting a card, overlaid with the text 'Market Power,' emphasizing the theme of financial influence. The video cuts back to Doug Kantor, then to a close-up of a green Visa card being swiped, followed by a retail scene where a cashier processes a payment at a counter with a 'TOP IT!' sign visible. A graphic from The Economist titled 'How Visa Became the Top Dog in Global Finance' appears, followed by a shot of a payment terminal with '$480 billion valuation' displayed. The video then shows a graph comparing Visa Inc.'s performance to the S&P 500, highlighting a '>170% gain in the past 5 years.' A montage of various Visa cards is shown, including a Wells Fargo Visa Debit card. The segment concludes with Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, speaking from a home setting with a fireplace and family photo in the background, wearing a dark floral blouse and gesturing as she speaks.

segments.caption: The video opens with a man in a black suit and red patterned tie, identified as Doug Kantor, speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting. The scene transitions to archival footage of an early automated teller machine (ATM), where a hand inserts a card into a Docutel machine, followed by a close-up of the screen prompting 'PLEASE ENTER YOUR PERSONAL IDENTIFICATION NUMBER.' The user then presses buttons on the keypad, selecting 'CASH FROM CHECKING' and entering a PIN. The narrative shifts to a modern setting, showing a woman with blonde hair, Lisa Ellis, speaking from a home environment with a fireplace and a framed family photo in the background. The video then cuts to an exterior shot of JPMorgan Chase & Co. at Two Seventy Park Avenue, followed by a top-down view of a woman using a modern outdoor ATM labeled 'BANK CREDIT GROUP.' The segment continues with a close-up of a hand swiping a card, overlaid with text stating '3.4 billion Visa cards in the market,' followed by a silhouette of a person using a tablet in front of a large digital screen displaying financial data. The video then shows a time-lapse of people walking with shopping bags, with text overlaying '$4.17 trillion in purchase volume in the U.S. during 2020.' This is followed by a close-up of a Mastercard, with text comparing Visa (3.4 billion cards) and Mastercard (2.3 billion cards), and then a comparison of U.S. purchase volumes in 2020: Visa at $4.17 trillion and Mastercard at $1.7 trillion. The segment concludes with a close-up of a chip card and returns to Doug Kantor speaking to the camera, reinforcing the financial narrative.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a payment terminal displaying logos for major credit card networks—American Express, Visa, MasterCard, Discover, and Novus—emphasizing the ubiquity of electronic payment systems. The scene transitions to a hand swiping a credit card through a terminal, overlaid with text highlighting Visa’s market presence and transaction volume. This is followed by a series of animated financial charts: first, a line graph showing Visa’s annual revenue growth from 2008 to 2020, rising from under $10B to over $20B; then, a comparison of annual stock returns between Visa Inc. (yellow line) and the S&P 500 (blue line) from 2009 to 2020, illustrating Visa’s volatility and strong performance; finally, a 2021 year-to-date stock return chart showing both Visa and S&P 500 trends up to October 15, 2021. The narrative continues with a person in a striped shirt analyzing financial documents and using a calculator, with on-screen text revealing Visa’s 2020 financials: $21.8 billion net revenue, $7.8 billion operating expenses, and $10.7 billion total net income. A silhouette scene of businesspeople shaking hands in a modern office is overlaid with text stating '30% to 40% profit margins.' The segment concludes with a man in a black suit and red patterned tie speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting, likely providing expert commentary on Visa’s financial performance.

segments.caption: The video opens with Lisa Ellis, a senior equity analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, speaking from her home setting, characterized by a fireplace mantel adorned with a silver platter, blue candles, and a framed family photo. She gestures expressively as she discusses Visa’s global presence. The scene transitions to a large outdoor billboard in Vietnam featuring a woman in a traditional conical hat, promoting Visa alongside other payment brands like MasterCard and Cirrus, with a Coca-Cola sign below. Next, a red promotional poster in China highlights Visa’s partnership with China Merchants Bank, offering discounts at brands like DKNY and Tommy Hilfiger, featuring a cartoon cat and QR code. The video then cuts to a time-lapse of people moving through the Mall of America, with escalators and storefronts visible, followed by a close-up of a Visa-branded poster at a Torino 2006 Olympics event, showing a Chinese speed skater. A payment terminal displays 'DECLINED' over a stack of U.S. currency and coins, symbolizing transaction failure. This is followed by a pie chart illustrating Visa’s gross revenue breakdown from its 2020 Annual Report: 39% from international transaction revenues, 34% from service revenues, 22% from data processing fees, and 5% from other revenues. An aerial view of a dense urban skyline, likely New York City, transitions back to Lisa Ellis, who continues her commentary. A CNBC article title appears: 'Visa takes a swipe in fintech, builds online marketplace,' dated May 26, 2021. The video then shows a hand holding a smartphone with a ride-hailing app interface indicating 'DRIVER EN ROUTE,' followed by a masked driver in a car, wearing gloves, suggesting safety during the pandemic. A person is seen using a laptop on a bed, possibly for online shopping or banking. The segment concludes with an exterior shot of the Visa headquarters, partially obscured by green foliage, reinforcing the company’s physical presence.

segments.caption: The video opens with a time-lapse shot of a modern, multi-level office building viewed through a large glass facade, showing people moving across staircases and walkways, with the text 'Relationship with merchants' appearing on screen. The scene transitions to Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting, wearing a black suit with a red patterned tie. This is followed by a close-up of a wooden gavel striking a sound block, symbolizing legal or judicial action. The next shot shows a hand inserting a card into an ATM, with the green light indicating acceptance. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of a classical government building with an American flag waving in the wind, suggesting a legal or regulatory context. Doug Kantor reappears, continuing his commentary. The video then cuts to a close-up of hands holding a credit card and using a smartphone, with gift boxes in the background, implying online shopping or payment. A text overlay from PR Newswire announces a '$5.54-6.24 Billion Settlement Providing Payments to Merchants Who Accepted Visa or Mastercard since 2004,' dated Feb. 22, 2019, displayed over an aerial view of a city. The final shot shows a hand inserting a card into a payment terminal at a retail checkout, with a blurred store background, emphasizing the transactional focus of the segment.

segments.caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a traditional bank interior, featuring large windows, wooden desks, and customers interacting with tellers, establishing a formal financial setting. This transitions to a CNBC news graphic announcing 'Visa abandons takeover of Plaid after DOJ raises antitrust concerns,' dated January 12, 2021, with a close-up of a gavel striking a sound block, symbolizing legal action. The scene shifts to a television screen showing a female news anchor delivering breaking news about the Visa-Plaid merger being called off, with the headline 'VISA, PLAID CALL OFF MERGER' displayed. The video then cuts to the exterior of the Department of Justice building, emphasizing the government's role, followed by a silhouette of Lady Justice holding scales and a sword, overlaid with a Bloomberg headline about Visa and Mastercard ATM access fee lawsuits receiving class certification. The narrative continues with a close-up of hands using an ATM, entering a PIN, and retrieving a card, with the text 'Visa declined to comment' appearing. This is followed by a cashier processing a payment at a retail checkout, then a close-up of a payment terminal with a card being swiped. The video then features Jared Scheeler, CEO of The Hub Convenience Stores, Inc., speaking directly to the camera in front of a bookshelf, with his name and title displayed. The final scenes show a low-angle view of a convenience store aisle with shoppers walking by, reinforcing the retail context of the payment systems discussed.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a payment terminal, focusing on its keypad and buttons, setting the stage for a discussion on payment processing fees. Text overlays appear sequentially, revealing that Visa and Mastercard collected $25.6 billion in swipe fees in 2009, rising to $67.6 billion in 2019, and then $110 billion in processing fees for all card payments in 2020, with sources cited as the National Retail Federation and HSN Consultants/Nilson Report. The visuals transition to a hand inserting a card into a terminal, followed by a man in a black suit with a red patterned tie—identified as Doug Kantor—speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting. The scene shifts to another man, Jared Scheeler, wearing a light blue collared shirt, speaking from a home office with a bookshelf behind him. The video then cuts to a person holding a smartphone and a credit card, preparing to make an online payment, with a laptop visible in the foreground. The sequence emphasizes the growth of card payment fees over time and includes expert commentary on the financial implications for merchants, blending data visualization with human insight.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a customer handing cash to a cashier at a grocery store checkout, with a bag of produce visible on the counter, emphasizing a traditional cash transaction. The scene transitions to Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, speaking from her home setting, with a fireplace and a framed family photo in the background; she gestures expressively as she discusses financial topics. The video then cuts to a series of close-ups showing hands counting and organizing U.S. currency in a cash register drawer, highlighting the physical handling of money. This is followed by a shot of a hand placing a $100 bill on a stack of cash, reinforcing the theme of cash transactions. The narrative shifts to a modern payment method, showing a customer using a smartphone to make a contactless payment at a restaurant, with a server holding a payment terminal. This is followed by a quick montage of various credit cards, including a black card with a chip, symbolizing digital payment options. The video then transitions to a CNBC graphic with the text 'Why wealthy Americans love AmEx,' accompanied by images of luxury shopping and a poolside scene, setting up a segment on American Express. The final frames display a bar chart titled 'Results for U.S. card networks at midyear (2022 purchase transactions),' showing Visa leading with $14.9 billion, followed by Mastercard at $6.6 billion, American Express at $3.5 billion, and Discover at $1.8 billion, sourced from the Nilson Report, 2022, and published in January 2023. The chart visually compares the transaction volumes of major U.S. card networks, underscoring Visa’s dominance in the market.

Next, Ted Rossman, Senior Industry Analyst from Bankrate.com and CreditCards.com, appears against a green background, wearing a black suit and blue tie, gesturing as he speaks. The video then shows a man in a suit using a smartphone to make a payment, followed by a woman in a suit using a smartphone to book a flight, and a person using a smartphone to search for 'New York Stock Exchange'. A line graph titled 'American Express Co (AXP:NYSE)' appears, showing revenue growth from 2013 to 2023, adjusted for inflation, with a blue line for American Express and a gray line for the S&P 500. Finally, Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, speaks from a home setting with a backdrop featuring 'svb Securities' and 'MoffettNathanson' logos, wearing a dark sleeveless top and a necklace, gesturing as she talks.

segments.caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of an American Express Tower, establishing the brand’s physical presence, followed by a close-up of a money-counting machine processing U.S. $100 bills, emphasizing financial volume. The scene transitions to a video call with Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, speaking from a home setting with 'svb Securities' and 'MoffettNathanson' logos in the background, wearing a dark sleeveless top and a necklace. Next, Dominick Gabriele, Executive Director and Senior Analyst at Oppenheimer & Co., appears on a laptop screen, speaking from a well-lit room with a bookshelf behind him. The video then shows a contactless payment transaction at a counter, where a customer uses a smartphone to tap a payment terminal, followed by a montage of various American Express cards, including gold, blue, and white designs, with the on-screen text 'What’s the secret to AmEx’s success?'. Archival black-and-white footage of a train car labeled 'Shipped by AMERICAN EXPRESS International Freight' is shown, with text overlays highlighting 'Closed-loop system', 'Revenue model', and 'Future'. A vintage American Express travel check is displayed, followed by Benji Stawski, CNBC Business News, speaking directly to the camera against a solid blue background. The video then shows a black-and-white archival scene of a man in a suit handing a card to a clerk in a tie store, followed by a close-up of a 1959 American Express credit card with 'John J Smith' as the cardholder. The segment concludes with a modern American Express card, a gold 'Centurion' card, and a final shot of the Man in Dark Suit and Glasses, speaking to the camera in a well-lit room with a window behind him.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a gold American Express card, highlighting its intricate design and the iconic Centurion logo, followed by a quick transition to a man in a dark suit and glasses speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit room with a window behind him. The scene shifts to a blue animated graphic illustrating the American Express closed-loop system, showing a circular flow between the cardholder and merchant, with American Express at the center. This is contrasted with an open-loop system diagram featuring Visa and Mastercard, where the cardholder bank and merchant bank are also involved, emphasizing the difference in transaction architecture. The video then cuts to a man in a black suit and blue tie speaking against a solid green background, gesturing as he explains the concepts. This is followed by a close-up of a payment terminal with a Mastercard being swiped, and then back to the man in the black suit continuing his explanation. segments.caption: The video opens with a group of three individuals—two men and a woman—engaged in a financial discussion at a sunlit outdoor table, with one man in a maroon blazer gesturing while holding documents. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and checkered shirt speaking directly to the camera in an office setting, followed by another man in a gray suit and blue patterned tie speaking against a solid orange background. The narrative then shifts to a bank interior, where a woman with a handbag interacts with a teller, and a man in a black suit leans over the counter. The video cuts to a series of expert interviews: Eli Peterson, Global Deputy General Counsel at TransUnion, appears in a home setting with a framed sky painting behind him; Sally Taylor, Vice President and General Manager at FICO, speaks from a room with beige curtains; and John Ulzheimer, President of The Ulzheimer Group, is shown in a living room with a fireplace and floral furniture. A close-up shows a woman’s hands using a smartphone calculator to compute figures on financial documents, with cash and glasses nearby. The scene returns to the outdoor group, now with the man in the maroon blazer handing papers to the others. A woman with curly hair and glasses is then shown reviewing documents at a kitchen table with a laptop, as the on-screen text 'How credit scores work in America' appears. The segment concludes with a graphic of a credit score meter ranging from 300 to 850, color-coded from red to blue, labeled 'Financial stability,' with the CNBC logo and text 'Credit scores' and 'Broken system? Changes' in the top left corner.

segments.caption: The video begins with a graphic titled 'Credit scores' from CNBC, displaying a horizontal bar ranging from 300 to 850, color-coded from red to blue to represent financial stability and creditworthiness. The bar transitions through labels indicating credit score ranges: 'Very bad credit score <580' in red, 'Sub-prime credit score 580-659' in orange, 'Average credit score 716' in green, and 'Elite credit score >720' in blue, with the source cited as the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. The graphic then shifts to a clipboard icon with a gauge and checklist, symbolizing credit report evaluation. The scene cuts to John Ulzheimer, President of The Ulzheimer Group, seated in a living room with a fireplace and floral furniture, wearing a light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera and gesturing with his hands. He appears to be explaining credit score categories, emphasizing points with hand movements. The video then transitions to a webpage screenshot of FICO® Scores, highlighting that 90% of top lenders use FICO® Scores, with statistics such as '10 Billion FICO® Scores are purchased every year' and '27 Million FICO® Scores are purchased every day,' reinforcing the widespread use and industry standard status of FICO® Scores. The CNBC logo remains visible throughout.

segments.caption: The video begins with a static infographic titled 'FICO® SCORE FACTS' from CNBC, highlighting that 10 billion FICO® Scores are purchased annually and 27 million daily, used by 90% of top lenders, and established as an industry standard for 30 years. This transitions to a clean, animated graphic of a blue credit card with a yellow chip, which then splits into segments to reveal the FICO credit score calculation: 35% Payment history, 30% Amount owed, 15% Length of credit history, 10% New credit, and 10% Types of credit. The scene shifts to Sally Taylor, Vice President and General Manager at FICO, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room with beige curtains. The video then cuts to a close-up of hands sorting financial documents on a wooden table, followed by a man in a blue shirt reviewing papers with a woman in a yellow top, with a Reuters headline overlay stating 'U.S. household debt tops $16 trillion amid rising inflation' dated August 2, 2022. segments.caption: The video begins with a static display of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 U.S.C. § 1681), revised in September 2018, highlighting a key provision in yellow that mandates credit reporting agencies to conduct a free reinvestigation within 30 days of a consumer dispute. This is followed by a bar chart titled 'CHART 2: Complaints to the CFPB about Credit Reporting Investigations Transmitted to the Credit Bureaus in 2017,' which shows that a majority of complaints (59% for Equifax, 64% for TransUnion, 63% for Experian) were that the investigation did not fix errors on the report. The scene then transitions to Chi Chi Wu, Staff Attorney at the National Consumer Law Center, speaking from her home office, which features bookshelves filled with books, decorative vases, framed photos, and a yellow vase. She gestures as she speaks, emphasizing her points. The video cuts to a close-up of hands using a calculator and pen on financial documents, suggesting financial analysis. Next, Aaron Klein, Senior Fellow at the Brookings Institution, appears in a gray suit with a blue patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera against a solid orange background, gesturing with his hands. The scene shifts to Eli Peterson, Global Deputy General Counsel at TransUnion, speaking from a home setting with a framed sky painting behind him, wearing a light blue suit and white shirt. The video then shows a close-up of a pen marking figures on a financial document, followed by a shot of hands using a calculator over a detailed financial statement with stacks of coins and cash in the background, reinforcing the theme of financial scrutiny and dispute resolution.
